summary: Martin argues that while computer hardware has improved by 22 orders of magnitude over 50 years, software development has remained fundamentally unchanged because abundant hardware power allowed developers to avoid revolutionary change. The approaching end of clock speed increases means future performance gains must come from massive parallelism—thousands of processors working together. Our current programming languages, closely tied to hardware abstractions, trap us in limited modes of thinking (the Sapir-Whorf trap). Martin suggests that functional programming, particularly Clojure/Lisp, offers a different enough mode of expression to potentially break us out of this conceptual prison and prepare us for the massively multicore future.
key_points: Hardware has improved 22 orders of magnitude in 50 years; software development methodology has barely changed because we could always rely on faster hardware
A 1960s software developer transported to 2011 could adapt in weeks; a hardware engineer would need to restart their career entirely
Programming languages closely tied to hardware (C, Java, C#) create a Sapir-Whorf trap that limits what concepts we can even conceive
The end of clock speed increases means future performance must come from massive parallelism—thousands of processors, not faster ones
Clojure/Lisp offers a sufficiently different mode of expression that might help break the conceptual constraints imposed by imperative languages
money_quotes: All that dramatic increase in hardware power has allowed us software people to avoid any similar major revolution.
We all still use the same old tools: sequence, selection, and iteration.
Our languages are projections of the hardware.
Concepts it can't express are removed from our awareness. Our mode of expression constrains us to only those thoughts and concepts that can easily be expressed within it.
In the coming decades, if you want your program to run fast, you are going to have to write it so that it shares thousands of processors. Not two. Not four. Thousands.
My fear is that these languages suffer from the Sapir/Whorf trap. Their mode of expression does not sufficiently change our world view.
Writing code in Lisp is different. It's a different mode of expression, and it makes you see concepts that are difficult, if not impossible, to conceive of in Java, or C.
key_insight: The abundant hardware power of the past 50 years has allowed software developers to avoid revolutionary change, but the shift to massive parallelism will force us to escape the conceptual prison of hardware-bound languages.
